data.title: Dogecoin Price Holds Rising Channel But Faces Pressure From Persistent Outflows
data.description:
By Parshwa Turakhiya

Dogecoin (DOGE-USD) is trading near $0.255 on Friday, consolidating within an ascending channel that has guided its price since June. The token’s broader structure remains constructive, but persistent outflows from exchanges highlight a fragile demand backdrop that has capped momentum in recent weeks.

On the daily chart, Dogecoin continues to respect higher lows within the rising channel, with the lower boundary near $0.24 providing immediate support. The cluster of exponential moving averages (20, 50, 100, and 200-day) between $0.22 and $0.25 reinforces this base, with the 200-day EMA at $0.221 acting as a structural safety net.

Resistance remains concentrated between $0.30 and $0.32, the zone that has repeatedly rejected rallies since August. A decisive breakout above this ceiling would confirm renewed bullish strength and potentially open the door toward $0.35 and $0.38, aligned with the upper boundary of the channel.

Momentum indicators are balanced, with the Relative Strength Index hovering at 54, leaving room for directional extension in either direction. The risk for bulls lies in a failure to hold above $0.24, which would invalidate the channel and expose deeper pullbacks toward $0.22 and $0.20.

Spot flows highlight fragile sentiment

Exchange flows have underscored the caution among investors. On October 3, Dogecoin recorded $38.6 million in net outflows, reflecting reduced exchange supply but also signaling that participants continue to move coins off trading platforms. While outflows can be interpreted as accumulation, the consistency of red prints through September reveals hesitation, with larger investors cutting exposure during rallies.

This persistent pattern helps explain why Dogecoin has struggled to sustain momentum beyond $0.30. Without strong inflows to balance out profit-taking, upside extensions have been repeatedly capped, keeping the asset locked within its channel range.

Derivatives and positioning

In derivatives markets, open interest has remained steady, suggesting active positioning, but leverage levels appear contained. Futures volume shows participation, but not yet the conviction needed to trigger an aggressive breakout. Options activity has been muted, further reflecting that traders are cautious in pressing directional bets until Dogecoin clears key resistance levels.

Outlook

Dogecoin’s near-term fate rests on its ability to hold the $0.24–$0.25 band. Sustaining this base keeps the door open for another attempt to challenge $0.30–$0.32, while a breakdown would put pressure on the $0.22 and $0.20 accumulation zones. For bulls, confirmation of a sustained rally requires improving spot inflows and stronger derivatives participation, without which upside momentum is likely to remain capped.

In earlier analysis, we highlighted Dogecoin’s structural resilience within its rising channel but noted the recurring struggle to build momentum above $0.30. That dynamic remains central, as improving technicals have not yet been matched by sufficient exchange flows or derivatives demand to break the consolidation.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Customers Bancorp: Upside Isn't Over Yet
data.description:
One of the downsides of having a hyper-concentrated portfolio is that you often miss out on some really stellar opportunities. A great example of this can be seen by looking at my rating history of Customers Bancorp, Inc. (NYSE:CUBI), an interesting bank with a market capitalization of $2.24 billion as of this writing. Back in March of this year, I reaffirmed it as a "Buy" candidate. This decision came even though asset quality at the time looked just decent as opposed to great and was driven primarily by balance sheet improvements and the fact that the stock was attractively priced.

Since that time, the company has definitely delivered, with shares jumping 21.7% compared to the 11.2% rise that the S&P 500 has enjoyed. And since I originally rated it a "Buy" in August 2023, the stock is up an astounding 90.4%. That's nearly double the 50.7% rise that the S&P 500 experienced. The data today suggest that we are seeing continued improvements, not only when it comes to the balance sheet, but when it comes to the income statement as well. Shares are cheap relative to earnings and are cheap compared to other similar firms. On top of this, asset quality is looking solid. Add on top of this different moves that management is making, and I think that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate is the right choice.

Still bullish

The newest data that we have regarding Customers Bancorp covers the second quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, the deposits for the institution came in at $18.98 billion. That is up from the $18.85 billion that the company ended 2024 with. It also compares favorably to the $18.16 billion the company had in 2023. In general, higher deposits are better than lower deposits. However, one thing I will say that I am not a fan of is the fact that a large amount of its deposits are uninsured. Management pegged that number at $7.4 billion, or approximately 39% of total deposits. I typically like to see uninsured deposit exposure of 30% or less. So this is certainly a knock against the company and is one of the reasons why I have not been more bullish on it than I have been.

The value of loans on its books, meanwhile, came in at $15.23 billion in the second quarter of this year. That's up from the $14.31 billion the company had at the end of last year. The greatest exposure that the company has involves specialized lending activities. These consist of direct finance and sales-type equipment leases, but they are overwhelmingly associated with fund finance, real estate specialty finance, technology and venture, health care, and other types of lending activities. Total exposure here came out to $6.45 billion in the most recent quarter. By comparison, the next largest category for the company would be the multifamily category, with $2.25 billion of its loans tied up there. Other major areas of focus include certain commercial and industrial loans, commercial real estate loans, residential real estate loans on the consumer side, and personal installment loans.

We should also pay attention to other parts of the balance sheet. Securities, for instance, amounted to $2.73 billion in the second quarter of this year. That's actually down from the $3.01 billion the company ended 2024 with. Meanwhile, cash and cash equivalents declined from $3.79 billion to $3.50 billion. And debt grew from $1.41 billion to $1.48 billion. What this all tells me is that management is focusing increasingly on pushing its assets into loans. And a lot of that growth seems to be on the specialized lending side of things. After all, back in December of last year, specialized lending loans amounted to just $5.84 billion. So in the span of just six months, we have seen a 10.5% jump on that front.

As the company's balance sheet increased and changed composition, we have also seen improvements in the income statement. In the second quarter of this year, for instance, net interest income for the company grew to $155.9 million. That's up from the $149.5 million reported the previous year. This was even though the firm's net interest margin dipped ever so slightly from 3.29% to 3.27% and was driven largely by the changes in the balance sheet. Things would have been even better had it not been for the fact that the provision for credit losses for the institution grew slightly from $18.1 million to $20.8 million. For the first half of this year, the $295.1 million that the company generated in net interest income beat out the $292.8 million reported the previous year. So the second quarter on its own did do a lot of heavy lifting to get this year up on a year-over-year basis.

Non-interest income, however, has experienced a lot of volatility. During the quarter, non-interest income amounted to just $29.6 million. That is down from the $31 million reported the previous year. But it is important to keep in mind that, in the second quarter of 2024, the firm booked an $11 million unrealized gain on equity method investments. For the first half of this year, things were even worse, with non-interest income of just $5.1 million compared to the $52.3 million reported the previous year. But this was mostly the result of a $51.3 million hit that the company took on certain debt securities. This caused overall net profits for the first half of this year to come in at only $65.4 million compared to the $100.2 million reported the previous year. And that would have been worse had it not been for the fact that, for the second quarter of this year, the company generated $55.8 million in net income compared to the $54.3 million reported at the same time last year.

If we adjust for this loss that the company took in debt securities, with the idea that these are in theory one-time events, we could see in the chart above that the company is trading at a price-to-earnings multiple of 7.8. That's comfortably below the 10 maximum that I like to see in this industry. It is also cheaper than any of the five companies that I compared it to in that chart. In the chart below, I did the same type of comparative analysis using the price-to-book and price-to-tangible book multiples. On a price-to-book basis, only one of the companies was cheaper than it is. And on a price-to-tangible-book basis, our candidate is the cheapest of the group.

Obviously, valuation is important. But it's also not the only thing we should be focused on. We should also be looking at asset quality. And on this side of things, Customers Bancorp really shines. In the first chart below, you can see the firm's return on assets. With a reading of 1.09%, it's above the 1% minimum threshold that I like to see. However, three of the five competitors are better than it is on this basis. The picture is even more attractive, however, when it comes to return on equity. In the subsequent chart, you can see what I mean. With a reading of 12.79%, Customers Bancorp is quite a bit higher than the 10% minimum I prefer. And it's also above all five companies that I stacked it up against.

There are a couple of other items that we should be touching on. One of these would be its credit quality. As the image below illustrates, there are different ways to look at this. But all of them look quite attractive if you ask me. At this time, for instance, only 0.27% of its assets are non-performing. Data provided by the FDIC shows that the average for a state Chartered Bank in Pennsylvania with assets of $1 billion or greater is 0.47%. FDIC data also shows that, for the same type of bank, 0.25% of all loans and leases are between 30 and 89 days past due. Although the image doesn't show this, for Customers Bancorp, I estimate that number is about 0.20%. So on the whole, credit quality looks solid.

I also like some other things that I'm seeing management do. For instance, in the final quarter of last year, the company launched a new platform called cubiX, which is essentially a bank-built platform that helps customers with real-time payment capabilities. Specifically, I am referring to commercial clients that are serviced. This is a massive market that the firm has previously serviced using an old platform. And after transitioning clients onto the new one, it's good to see that total annual payment volume using data from 2024 amounted to $1.5 trillion. This, combined with other developments in the space, such as the GENIUS Act, makes this not only a bank but also a financial technology firm with growth potential.

Takeaway

Based on all the data currently available, I must say that I remain bullish about Customers Bancorp. Honestly, I regret never buying shares of the company. The firm remains attractively priced even after skyrocketing. Add on top of this its asset quality and continued growth, and I believe that maintaining it as a "Buy" candidate makes sense.

data.title: Interest Rate Implications From U.S. Government Shutdown
data.description:
While the market is shrugging off the potential impact of the U.S. government shutdown, some investors are concerned a prolonged shutdown could prompt the U.S. Federal Reserve to rethink the path of future rate cuts. Gennadiy Goldberg, Head of U.S. Rates Strategy with TD Securities, joins MoneyTalk to discuss how investors and the US Fed are navigating the increased uncertainty.

Transcript

Anthony: The U.S. government shutdown has entered its second day with no clear end in sight. Is there potential for the disruption to have an impact on the economy and interest rates? Joining us now to discuss is Gennadiy Goldberg, head of U.S. rate strategy with TD Securities. And Gennadiy, welcome to the show. Thanks for joining us.

Gennadiy Goldberg: Thanks for having me.

Anthony: All right, so how damaging could this shutdown be on the U.S. economy?

Gennadiy Goldberg: Well, historically, markets have largely tended to ignore these mostly because the impact on the economy has been relatively mild. Now, historically, shutdowns have also been relatively short. The longest one was actually about 34 days in President Trump's first term. And that, obviously, took some time to get through. But even the impact of that was relatively modest. I mean, we're thinking about maybe 0.2 percentage points off the GDP for the quarter. That's kind of the levels that we're talking about. So really, the underlying fundamentals still matter a lot more for markets. But really this creates an extra layer of risk for markets and for economists.

Anthony: All right, now one of the big focus of the markets have been interest rates and potential cuts by the Federal Reserve. What impact will that have on interest rates and Fed policy going forward?

Gennadiy Goldberg: Yeah, I mean, this is the trickiest part. Because we have the shutdown, a lot of the government data is not being released. So, for example, Friday's payroll report will not come out. We didn't get jobless claims this morning. So we really kind of are flying a little bit half-blind. Now, we do have private sector data. So, for example, we got the ADP report. We did get some of the regional Fed surveys as well. But it really is only half the picture. So the way I would put it is the fog is just getting thicker. We still expect the Fed to cut rates in October and December and three more times next year. But it really makes their decisions fairly tricky, even though the October rate cut is actually largely priced into markets for now.

Anthony: All right, and, of course, we talked about equity markets have shrugged off much of that news in Wednesday's session. Do you expect investors to continue to be somewhat upbeat about the situation going forward?

Gennadiy Goldberg: I don't know if it's upbeat. It's more so kind of a cautious optimism, I would say. Most investors are looking at this and saying, look, we're not really seeing the impact on the economy. If we do see it, it'll be relatively modest. Rates investors at the same time are also kind of chopping around in a very narrow range. You're not really seeing much of an impact. The big question for us is, really, what's the lasting impact here? The one caveat here is the shutdown could be a little bit different. The second Trump term has been different from just about any other presidency in living memory. And Trump has threatened to, instead of furloughing employees, so basically laying them off temporarily, to permanently fire federal workers. And that could actually have some market implications over the long run if it starts to push up things like the unemployment rate, for example. What we don't know is the extent of those plans, how much of that is a negotiating tool. And really, Anthony, the big question is just how long this lasts. The longer this lasts, the bigger the impact.

Anthony: That's right. And you talked about jobs and the impact on the labour market, and the Fed seems to have pivoted towards the weakness of the labour market. Of course, because of this strike -- or because of what's happening in the U.S., there's no jobs report on Friday. What data is going to be available that you'll be keeping an eye on?

Gennadiy Goldberg: Yeah, I mean, it's all the private sector numbers. So we got the ISM manufacturing report. We'll get the ISM services report on Friday as well. That's really the only one. And the ADP number could be a little bit more of a miscue than a cue. If you look at the headline number, which is actually showing a contraction in jobs for the first time in a long time, that's certainly weaker. But if you look at the non-seasonally adjusted data, which is what we use as kind of a sanity check for payrolls, that actually came in a little bit better. So net net, we still don't know. We're still kind of in a fog of war. But the market's kind of using what it can get, not necessarily what it wants to get. So we're just kind of scrounging around so far. Keep in mind, the Fed has their own internal data. They do use some of the private sector data to kind of create their own views, but the gold standard is still the BLS, the BEA of the Census Bureau, and all of those, unfortunately, are shuttered at the moment.

Original Post

data.title: YMAG Has Found The Sweet Spot, But It Won't Last
data.description:
I recently published an article outlining why distributions in the YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (YMAX) had just made a new record low, and despite some short-term fluctuations, the trend in payouts is clearly down. To summarize, it is due to a combination of lower volatility, lower NAV, and a change in strategy that attempts to slow NAV erosion.

The YieldMax Magnificent 7 Fund of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAG) is similar to YMAX, as it is also a "fund of funds" with some overlap in holdings. I, therefore, expected a similar trend lower in its distributions. However, the distributions are much more variable, and it is very hard to distinguish a clear pattern. Furthermore, NAV has now stabilized, unlike the resumption of the trend lower in YMAX in recent months. This article looks at why YMAG has found the "sweet spot" where both the yield and NAV are steady and why it could be hard to sustain.

YMAG Basics

As mentioned in the intro, YMAG is a "fund of funds" that holds the YieldMax Magnificent 7 Income funds in equal weighting.

These ETFs all have the same basic strategy, which is to write covered calls to provide income and provide exposure to the underlying stocks through a variety of methods (more on that later).

Unlike YMAX, we do have a benchmark against which to compare the YMAG performance. The Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF (MAGS) has exposure to the same underlying stocks, again at equal weighting. A total return comparison shows YMAG is underperforming by around 31% since it was launched in January 2024.

This underperformance is very common in income funds using the covered call strategy. The Mag 7 stocks have strong uptrends, and the strategy limits participation in the rallies. Its main attraction is the "juicy" yield and weekly payouts.

A High But Variable Distribution

The juicy distribution shown above fluctuates as each weekly payment is variable. The changes can be quite extreme, and I have highlighted a recent low of $0.0554 and a high of $0.2929.

The payments are dictated by whatever YMAG receives from its holdings in the other Mag 7 ETFs. These pay out monthly at different times throughout the month, so some weekly YMAG payments will contain distributions from two (or more) funds, while some weeks it will only be one.

The size of the distributions is also affected by volatility. A covered call strategy sells calls and collects the premium. The higher the volatility, the higher the premium. We can see that distributions were clearly higher in May of this year, when tariff news caused large moves in the Mag 7 stocks.

Distributions can also change in line with NAV – the higher the NAV, the more assets the fund has and the higher the payments.

Lastly, distributions are reliant on a particular ETF's strategy, as management can decide to focus on income or try to participate more in the underlying stock's rally. This is a relatively new situation arising from tweaks to the prospectus in February.

When all these factors are taken together, it is difficult to ascertain if there is a pattern or trend in the distribution size. There is a tendency to get one or two smaller distributions followed by two larger ones, but this is a function of the payout dates in the underlying ETFs.

The main takeaway is that distributions are not going down like they are in YMAX. We can calculate the average distribution after May as $0.143, and the average before May (excluding the large first payment of the year) is $0.11, so there may even be a slight rise.

NAV Matters

YMAG has experienced sharp NAV erosion, even when the Mag 7 stocks are performing well.

However, it has been quite stable since May.

It's tempting to say this is due to the YieldMax funds' new strategy. This is from the updated summary prospectus of one of the funds YMAX holds, YieldMax TSLA Option Income Strategy ETF (TSLY):

Additional Investment Strategies

In addition to the options strategies described herein, the Fund may use the following additional strategies to gain exposure to the Underlying Security:

● Swaps: The Fund may utilize swap contracts that provide exposure to the Underlying Security.

● Additional Options Strategies: The Fund may utilize additional options strategies, including purchasing call options that are “deep in the money” (i.e., the Underlying Security’s price is well above the strike price on the call option).

● Equity Securities: The Fund may purchase equity securities of the Underlying Security.

There are 3 new ways to gain exposure to the rally in the underlying instrument. If management chooses to do this, it could have a positive impact on the NAV but a negative effect on distributions. It's a balance, and it can't really be both.

I think the strategy change is a step forward for the fund. It should mean NAV is not on a permanent downward spiral. However, the verdict is still out if it can keep the NAV and distributions as stable as they have been over the last 6 months. Don't forget, YMAG is very dependent on the Mag 7 stocks, and these have been trending strongly higher.

This strong trend just happens to have taken place since the YMAG strategy changes took place, although you could say the Mag 7 tends to trend higher in most periods. In any case, the strong performance has helped YMAG keep its NAV and distributions balanced and steady.

However, if the Mag 7 were to trade sideways, it would be a new challenge for YieldMax – would they try to maintain a steady yield or try to keep NAV steady? I suspect they would try to do both, and that would mean NAV trends down but at a slower pace, while distributions take a small hit. I think that's better than what was happening before in the period of July 2024 to April 2025, when NAV fell sharply.

Conclusions

Over the last six months, YMAG has found the "sweet spot" where the NAV is steady and distributions are also steady and perhaps even rising. While I'd love to see this continue and for the strategy change to lead to a long-term stable NAV, I am doubtful it will. The Mag 7 will, at some point, stop rallying strongly and even consolidate/drop for a period, in which case, YMAG will struggle to keep paying 50% yields without eroding the NAV. Either the distributions will come down, or the NAV will.

data.title: First American Financial: Attractive Even With Muted Growth (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of First American Financial Corporation (NYSE:FAF) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 3% of their value. With long-term rates staying elevated, mortgage activity has been muted. Rates have not been low enough to spur much refinancing activity, while strained affordability limits home purchases. Rate cuts could potentially help activity, and the commercial side of the business has seen more momentum. I last covered shares in April, when I upgraded FAF to a “Hold.” In hindsight, I should have remained a "Sell" as its 9% rally significantly underperformed the market’s 25% gain. With updated financials and a changing macro outlook, now is a good time to revisit FAF.

Rates have limited mortgage demand

First American Financial is a leading title insurer. Title insurance volumes are tied closely to the level of mortgage originations, as typically a borrower needs to buy title insurance when taking out a mortgage—this applies to both purchases and refinancing. The lackluster level of housing transactions has been an impediment to the business. As you can see below, existing home sales have been stuck at extremely depressed levels for a prolonged period of time.

Given how low volumes are, I do not see incremental downside from here, even if the economy were to soften further. There have been multiple challenges to home sales. First, the combination of elevated rates and prices has made housing relatively unaffordable, reducing demand. Additionally, owners with low-rate mortgages are very hesitant to sell their homes, as they do not want to give up their 3.5% or lower mortgage rate. Together, these factors have frozen the housing market. If mortgage rates continue to fall, that can help reduce these headwinds, though weakening employment would be a new headwind. While I do not see volumes falling, I also do not view a material acceleration over the next 6-12 months as likely either.

The key driving factor will be what happens to mortgage rates. Ultimately, 30-year mortgage rates are more closely tied to the 10-year Treasury than the federal funds rate, which is why last year’s Fed rate cuts did not sustainably lower mortgage costs. Mortgage rates have spent much of the past three years stuck in the 6.5%-7.25% range, though we did recently break lower. I believe a drop below 6% is what is really necessary to ignite a significant refinancing wave beyond levels we currently see, as typically borrowers need a 75-100bp rate cut to make a refinancing worth the cost. Sub-6% rates could also spur more purchase volumes. Given long-term rates are already discounting meaningful Fed rate cuts, I am skeptical we will see mortgage rates fall that much further.

Instead, I view volumes as likely to remain similar to recent levels, though First American is doing a better job of maintaining margins at this level of mortgage activity. In the company’s second quarter report on July 23, First American Financial earned $1.53 per share, which beat estimates by $0.13 as revenue grew 14% to $1.8 billion. The company generated a 13.2% title margin, up 130bps from last year, as past cost-cutting efforts begin to show benefits. On the last earnings call, management said it expects the pace of margin expansion to moderate as we lap some of these actions.

Commercial strength leads to results

In total, title revenue grew by 13% to $1.7 billion. Personnel costs were up a more modest 8% to $523 million as it more fully utilized its staff and enjoyed operating leverage associated with higher revenue. Purchase revenue was down 3% from last year given the weak existing home sales environment I discussed earlier. In contrast, we have seen some benefit from rate declines as refinancing revenue rose 54% thanks to a 44% jump in volume.

While the residential side of the business is still soft, we are seeing genuine green shoots in commercial activity. Commercial revenue grew by 33%, led by strength in multifamily. Commercial revenue was $234 million. Volumes rose 2% with pricing up 30% given larger average deal sizes. Refinancings accounted for 46% of volume. It sees refinancing strength continuing through next year, given the maturity profile. During 2023-2024, capital markets access for commercial real estate borrowers was very difficult, and now that markets have loosened, there is meaningful pent-up demand to refinance debt and extend maturities. I do see growth sustaining here.

The company has more recently provided interim Q3 data, which showed a continuation of Q2 themes. Through August, purchase orders per day are down about 5% from last year, with July particularly weak. In contrast, refi orders were up about 50%. In August, commercial orders reached the highest in any month since 2022. In Q4, refinancing comparisons will be much more difficult, and I would expect to see growth moderate from here.

Underwriting quality remains strong, and title insurance is a very low-loss form of insurance, given the excellent record-keeping of property ownership in the United States. FAF took 3% reserves, using a 3.75% reserve on new policies and a $10 million release given strong historic performance. I expect it to continue to use 3.75% on new policies with further reserve releases likely.

Aside from the title, FAF has a small home warranty unit. Here, revenue was up 3% to $110 million, but pre-tax profits jumped 35% to $22 million. First American had a strong home warranty margin of 20.7%, as claims frequency declined, resulting in a 5% lower claims rate of 41%. I do not see margins expanding beyond this level.

While elevated rates reduce title demand, they do help boost investment income on its $10.8 billion of cash and investments, providing some hedge inside the business. Indeed, investment income rose $21 million to $147 million. With ongoing Fed rate cuts, I expect to see declines starting in Q4 as cash yields fall.

With a solid balance sheet and fair valuation, FAF is attractive

FAF has a strong balance sheet with $1.55 billion of debt, resulting in 23.1% debt to capital. I like to see FAF operate sub-25%, so with its current balance sheet, it can focus cash flow on shareholder returns. Management is doing just that. In September, First American Financial increased its dividend by 2% to $0.55, giving shares a 3.4% forward yield. It is also accelerating buyback activity with $61 million of buybacks in Q2 and a new $300 million authorization. Its share count was down 0.5%, and I expect the share count reduction to accelerate in the coming quarters.

Back in April, I was targeting $4.40-$4.70 in EPS this year. However as capital markets have loosened, commercial volume has exceeded my expectations, and FAF’s incremental margins have been stronger than I hoped for. As such, I now see EPS running at ~$5.15-$5.40 this year. I still view ~13x as the right multiple for the business given its solid balance sheet and the low-loss nature of title insurance for a fair value of ~$69.

With 8% upside and a 3.5% dividend yield, investors have a low-double-digit total return opportunity, and I believe recent underperformance has made FAF sufficiently cheap to merit an upgrade to a “Buy.” In a market where many stocks have elevated valuations, FAF is a rare quality company at a reasonable valuation, making shares attractive for value-oriented investors.

data.title: JQC: Heavy Leverage Use And Generous Payouts Limit Appeal
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered Nuveen Credit Strategies Income Fund (NYSE:JQC), I issued a Sell rating due to the questionable distribution coverage and premium valuation. Since the time of my last coverage, the macroeconomic environment has shifted and the fund has released an updated annual report. With markets continuing to trade near all-time highs, investors may be looking for a defensive income fund that can offset the uncertainty that traditional equities expose investors to. Now that the first interest rate cut for 2025 has been implemented, the fund may see some relief in terms of its operating spread between the cost of leverage and yield collected from securities.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that JQC's share price has declined by about 7.4%. The fund was able to participate in some positive momentum over the last few months but is still down for the year. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps to 4.15% over the same time frame. JQC now offers investors a starting dividend yield of about 12.2%. Payouts are issued on a monthly basis, which makes the fund an enticing choice for income investors. However, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of payouts at this time.

According to the latest annual report, JQC's distribution coverage still remains inconsistent. However, management may have strategically raised the distributions as a way to help shrink the fund's discount pricing. JQC has consistently traded at a large double-digit discount over the last decade, so a larger payout may help bring the fund's share price in line with its fair value. However, there haven't been any official updates provided by management so it's hard to determine whether or not payouts will be sustained once the fund's discount is entirely eliminated.

Fund Strategy

According to the fund's latest overview, JQC has total managed assets of $1.32B that are spread across 447 different individual holdings. The fund chooses to primarily invest in senior loans and CLO debt because of their reduced interest rate risk. The fund specifically aims to invest a significant portion of its assets into securities that have below investment-grade credit ratings. The tradeoff is that higher yields can be extracted from these lower-rated securities. The fund's breakdown by asset type is as follows:

  • Senior Loans: 71.2%.
  • Corporate Bonds: 20.0%.
  • Collateralized Loan Obligation Debt: 5.0%.
  • Cash & Equivalents: 3.5%.
  • Net Other Assets: 1.5%.
  • Common Stocks: 0.5%.

To help mitigate the risks, JQC allocates its assets across a diverse range of sectors to reduce concentration. As we can see below, JQC is most heavily allocated to Information Technology securities at 16.2% of assets. This is followed by exposure to Consumer Discretionary and Industrial securities, which account for 14.6% and 13.8% of assets respectively. The combination of these top three sectors makes up over 44% of assets, so these sectors may have the greatest impact on JQC's daily price change and total return potential.

When looking at the fund's top positions, we can see that JQC is most heavily allocated to private investments. For instance, the only public position that I can see within the top holdings is CommScope Holding Company, Inc. (COMM). It's important to remember that JQC prioritizes income generation, so I believe that investors should limit their expectations around the fund's ability to provide a meaningful level of capital appreciation over time.

Just to demonstrate this weakness, I pulled in a performance chart for the last decade. As we can see below, JQC's share price has declined by about 32.7% over the last ten years. JQC has failed to participate in many of the market rallies over the last decade. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 93.86% over the same period. Therefore, investors should expect that most of the fund's returns will come from its ability to continue paying out large distributions.

Updated Valuation & Risk Profile

At the time of my last coverage, JQC traded at a discount to NAV of 3.6%. Despite the market rally over the last few months, JQC now trades at a slightly larger discount to NAV of approximately 6.55%. For reference, JQC has traded at an average discount to NAV of 8.39% over the last five-year period. To better conceptualize the current discount level in relation to its historical range over the last ten years, I added the red line to the graph below. As we can see, the discount levels have drastically decreased over the last ten years, and I believe this may be due to the fact that JQC has consistently paid out more in distributions than it actually generated in income.

JQC has also released an updated annual report for 2025. Net investment income amounted to $0.48 per share, while the fund experienced net realized losses of $0.08 per share. Looking back over the last few years, we can see that net investment income levels have slightly decreased from its prior years. This may be attributed to the fact that a portion of JQC's securities are structured on a floating rate basis. Since interest rates are slightly lower than they were in prior years, the level of net investment income has been negatively impacted. Additionally, net investment income levels aren't high enough to support distribution on their own.

This means that the fund is ultimately reliant on positive net realized gains to make up the difference in earnings. The problem is that net realized gains are hard to predict since they are dependent on positive market conditions, which allow JQC to sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. This reliance has caused JQC's NAV growth to remain inconsistent over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $6.91 per share.
  • 2022: $6.10 per share.
  • 2023: $5.83 per share.
  • 2024: $5.91 per share.
  • 2025: $5.66 per share.

As part of the fund's operating strategy, JQC utilizes leverage in an effort to amplify earnings when possible. The use of leverage currently amounts to 37.14% of assets, which is equivalent to about $494.6M. I consider this to be an aggressive amount of leverage in this higher interest rate environment. Investors need to also consider the risks that leverage can amplify losses if allocated to securities that don't perform as anticipated. So although JQC specifically tries to limit the sensitivity to interest rates, the high use of leverage leads to JQC's share price remaining suppressed.

A higher interest rate environment means that it's more costly to utilize leverage. For instance, JQC's annualized cost of leverage now sits at 5.61%. Therefore, the fund's operating spread between the cost of its debt and the yield collected from securities may be thinner. As we can see below, this has caused JQC's share price to remain suppressed since interest rates were increased to their decade highs throughout 2022 and 2023.

Even though the Fed has implemented a small 25 basis point cut recently, the federal funds rate still remains high. Since JQC has a majority exposure to securities that have poor credit ratings, the fund is exposed to the possibility of defaults within its holdings. For instance, S&P Global estimates that leveraged loan defaults can rise as high as 3.5% by March of 2026. This assumes continued trade disruption related to tariffs and slower economic growth.

Just for reference, a borrower with a credit rating below BBB is considered to be below investment grade. These are typically borrowers that have less consistent cash flows or may be more reliant on debt to fuel operational growth. The fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 11.2%.
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 88%.
  • Not Rated: 0.8%.

Questionable Dividend Sustainability

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.054 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.2%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of approximately $0.648 per share. Referring back to the same annual report for 2025, we can see that JQC has a mixed history of being able to generate a sufficient level of earnings that can actually support the payouts that it makes. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $0.88 per share.
  • 2022: ($0.33) per share.
  • 2023: $0.28 per share.
  • 2024: $0.72 per share.
  • 2025: $0.40 per share.

As we can see, JQC has only been able to generate a sufficient level of earnings in two of the last five years. This doesn't instill confidence that payouts can be sustained if the fund is frequently paying out more than it generates in income. However, management has continued to increase dividends over the last three years. I believe that the higher payouts may have been implemented as a way to reduce the fund's discount to NAV valuation and get the fund back near its fair value. Since the discount window is trending smaller, it's hard to determine whether or not distributions will remain this high in the future.

Additionally, it's worth noting that the distributions received from JQC are typically funded by net investment income, which has the highest tax burden for investors. According to the latest section 19(a) notice, most of the payouts on a YTD basis were made up of net investment income. So based on your specific tax bracket, desired income level, or the amount of capital invested, a position in JQC may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my Sell rating on JQC at this time due to the higher interest rate environment. The fund's heavy use of leverage ultimately limits the appeal, in addition to the generous payouts that may not be sustained if the fund isn't capable of generating positive net realized gains. The NAV still maintains its downtrend and the fund's structure is likely to continue eroding investor capital over time. So although JQC offers a high dividend yield, I do not believe the value proposition is attractive at this time.

data.title: U.S. Business Surveys Strengthen The Case For Rate Cuts
data.description:
By James Knightley

Services activity cooled as hiring slides

The government shutdown means no 'official' economic data, but we continue to get numbers from other organisations. Today’s ISM services index for September has come in weaker than predicted, dropping from 52 (growth territory) to 50 (consistent with flat activity). The consensus prediction was 51.7, but the outcome was actually below all individual survey responses provided to Bloomberg.

The details show business activity dropping to 49.9 from 55.0. This is the worst outcome since the shutdown period of the pandemic in May 2020, while new orders fell from 56.0 to 50.4. The employment component rose to 47.2 from 46.5, but because it remains below 50, this increase merely means that the pace of job losses slowed last month. The chart below shows the output measures of both the services and manufacturing ISM series versus annual GDP growth, and based on the historical relationship, points to the risk of slowing growth in the coming quarters.

GDP growth (YoY%) versus ISM output metrics advanced 6 months

Weaker jobs trumps inflation worries

While we didn’t get the jobs report today, the ADP private payrolls numbers earlier in the week suggest the jobs market continues to cool, while the job openings numbers within the JOLTS report show there are now more unemployed people in America than there are job vacancies. At the same time, a slowing quits rate – a measure of job turnover – is pointing to wage growth dropping below 3% in early 2026. This combination of sub-trend growth and weakening jobs numbers will, we believe, drive the Fed’s interest rate decisions.

There are lingering concerns about tariffs pushing up prices and inflation, with today’s ISM prices paid series doing nothing to dispel them – it rose to 69.4 from 69.2, so well above the 50 break-even level. However, tariffs have come through more slowly than feared in the key inflation metrics the Fed focuses on, of CPI and the PCE deflator. As such, the balance of risks to the Fed’s dual mandate of price stability and maximum employment justifies the central bank moving monetary policy closer to neutral with 25bp interest rate cuts at the October and December FOMC meetings expected.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Platinum Could Have Substantial Upside Potential: Reasons To Consider The PLTM ETF
data.description:
After years of trading in a tight consolidation range between just below $800 and just under $1,200 per ounce, NYMEX platinum futures broke out to the upside in June 2025. The $1,000 pivot point gave way to a rally that has, so far, taken platinum futures to the highest price since 2013 at over $1,600 per ounce.

The GraniteShares Platinum Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:PLTM) tracks platinum’s price and is slightly less expensive than the abrdn Physical Platinum Shares ETF (PPLT).

Platinum prices have exploded

NYMEX platinum futures broke out of an over decade-long consolidation pattern in June 2025.

The twenty-year continuous monthly NYMEX platinum futures chart highlights the 75.7% decline from the 2008 record high of $2,308.80 per ounce to the March 2020 pandemic-inspired low of $562.00 per ounce. Excluding the March 2020 low, platinum futures traded on either side of the $1,000 per ounce pivot point from 2015 through May 2025. Each rally was a selling opportunity, while price weakness below the pivot point was a buying opportunity. In June 2025, platinum futures rose above the February 2021 high and critical technical resistance of $1,348.20 per ounce. In late September, futures rose to the highest price since 2013, surpassing $1,650, and were above $1,640 per ounce on October 3.

More upside if gold and silver are models

Precious metals are following gold, the leader of the precious pack, which has been in a bullish trend since the 1999 low.

The quarterly COMEX gold futures chart highlights gold’s bullish trend since the 1999 low of $252.80 per ounce. Gold surpassed the high from 1980 at $875 in early 2008 and eclipsed the $1,000 level for the first time the same year. Gold rose above $2,000 for the first time in 2020 and rose to over $3,000 in 2025. Since 2024, gold’s ascent has turned parabolic, with futures trading above $3,900 per ounce as of early October 2025. As the chart shows, gold rose to a new record high over the past nine consecutive quarters, including the fourth quarter of 2025.

Silver has recently joined the bullish party.

While COMEX silver futures have yet to challenge the 2011 high of $49.82 and the 1980 high of $50.36 per ounce, the price has taken off on the upside since the March 2020 pandemic-inspired low of $11.64. At over $48.25 per ounce in early October, silver is within striking distance of the highs from 2011 and 1980.

Gold, silver, and platinum have industrial and financial applications. The rallies in gold and silver support higher platinum prices.

Platinum remains a rare and illiquid metal

Gold and silver are highly liquid metals:

  • Each COMEX gold futures contract contains 100 ounces. The total open interest in the gold futures market at 513,357 contracts on September 26 means that $3,860 per ounce on the December 2025 futures contract translates to over $198.155 billion market value. Gold futures trade an average of approximately 250,000 contracts daily.
  • Each COMEX silver futures contract contains 5,000 ounces. The total open interest in the silver futures market at 169,643 contracts on September 26 means that $47 per ounce on the December 2025 futures contract translates to over $39.866 billion market value. Silver futures trade an average of approximately 90,000 contracts daily.
  • Each NYMEX platinum futures contract contains 50 ounces. The total open interest in the platinum futures market at 95,235 contracts on September 26 means that $1,640 per ounce on the January 2026 futures contract translates to under $7.81 billion market value. Platinum futures trade an average of approximately 50,000 contracts daily.

Lower daily volume and open interest data highlight that platinum futures are far less liquid than gold and silver futures. Lower liquidity can lead to higher price volatility as price gaps are common in less liquid futures markets. Therefore, while platinum futures have moved significantly higher, the potential for an upside price gap is increasing in the current bullish environment. Moreover, while gold continues to reach new record highs and silver is within striking distance of its all-time peak price, platinum, at over $650 below its October 2008 high, has room to rise.

PLTM is a platinum ETF product

The fund profile for theGranite Shares Platinum Trust ETF states:

PLTM’s top holdings include:

As the chart shows, PLTM does an excellent job tracking platinum’s price because it owns physical platinum bullion.

At $15.56 per ounce, PLTM had $130.83 million in assets under management. PLTM trades an average of nearly 500,000 shares daily and charges a 0.50% management fee. PLTM’s expense ratio is 0.10% lower than that of its competitor, PPLT.

The chart shows that the NYMEX continuous platinum futures contract rallied 88.4% from the April 7, 2025, low of $878.30 to the September 30, 2025, high of $1,655.00 per ounce.

Over the same period, PLTM rallied 77.2% from $8.76 to $15.52 per share. The differential is a function of the expense ratio, and because platinum futures trade around the clock, the PLTM ETF is only available during U.S. stock market hours. PLTM can miss highs or lows in the platinum market when the U.S. stock market is closed.

Seeking Alpha ETF Grades Favor PLTM

PLTM’s Seeking Alpha ETF Grades are:

As the chart shows, platinum’s rally and the PLTM’s upward trajectory earn the highest possible A+ grade. PLTM receives a B+ in liquidity, while the C+ in expenses is a function of the management fee. The PPLT ETF, with a higher 0.60% expense ratio receives a C- expense grade. PLTM earns an A- in liquidity due to its assets and daily trading volume.

While the continuous platinum futures rallied 88.4% from the April low to the September high, and PLTM moved 77.2%, PPLT moved 77.5% higher from $82.79 to $146.94 per share over the same period.

Platinum’s price could have significant upside potential over the coming weeks and months if gold and silver serve as models. Platinum continues to offer value compared to its precious cousins. PLTM is available for standard equity accounts and allows for exposure to metals' price action without owning the bullion. The expense ratio covers the storage and insurance costs. I remain bullish on platinum in early October 2025, expect higher prices, and rate the PLTM ETF a buy.

data.title: Middle Coast Investing Q3 2025 Letter: Artificial Intelligence, Natural Caution
data.description:
The third quarter was a very fortunate one for Middle Coast Investing. Our collective portfolio rose ahead of the S&P 500 and is ahead of our benchmarks year to date. Buyouts of four portfolio companies, including two significant positions, provided a big boost. Several other stocks jumped in price, for reasons that matched our investment theses. Only two stocks dropped meaningfully. And we managed these results without overextending ourselves. Before discussing those results, though, I’d like to cover a topic that has been on the market’s mind for 2+ years, but that I’ve only touched upon a little: Artificial Intelligence.

When a person with the slightest shred of interest in investing learns what I do, they ask: ‘how are you investing in artificial intelligence?’ More than crypto, software, or any other trends or fads I’ve known in my 14 years of investing, artificial intelligence has captured public attention. It of course has also been a major economic force, and raises a lot of questions about our market and the future of our economy.

I believe two things about AI are most relevant to Middle Coast Investing clients and future clients.

  1. How we are trying to use it for our business
  2. How we invest in it

Using AI in the business

Middle Coast Investing’s business can be broken into three components: the investing process, administration, and marketing. We’ll set aside marketing – that is its own problem that doesn’t affect current clients. The most pressing area for me to improve efficiency is in my administration process. Automation of any form would be valuable in tracking performance, transactions, balances, and billing.

AI is a broad term, though over the past few years generative AI, ala ChatGPT, Google’s Gemini, and so on, has been the most common reference. GenAI is usually an application built on a large language model that produces text in a natural, conversational way.

I’m less confident of AI’s ability to handle numbers and math properly, as we’ll see below. My initial line of inquiry has been to use Gemini as a guide to how to get smarter with Excel. It has given me a few small ideas and one potentially bigger one - the query tool that might save me big step. It has been an unsatisfying experience so far, but I need to invest more time. There are more ways to save time and automate processes. But it’s not clear to me if the solution is ‘find a way to plug AI tricks in’ or ‘just go learn how to use Excel properly.’

AI in Investment Research

Investment research and decision making is the core to Middle Coast Investing. It behooves us to consider any tool that can make that process better. It’s easy to puff oneself up and say finding stocks and making investment decisions is a human art form, one that can’t be replicated by computers. That’s obviously untrue – quantitative hedge funds have been around for decades – but also irrelevant.

I don’t intend to outsource decision making. But there are tedious parts of the research process that we are doing manually, and would like to automate.

For example, compiling data about a stock. 3rd party sources often use non-standard definitions for financial figures. I prefer to pull information from a company’s SEC filings. I tried using Gemini to do this for me.

I started with Business A, a recently spun-off company. I asked Gemini to give me the numbers for a variety of categories: shares outstanding, market cap, book value, trailing twelve months revenue, etc.

Gemini first pointed out that for the spun off company it’s difficult, since it is a new company with less than a year of 10-Ks and 10Qs. Fair enough. I asked Gemini to give me a list of competitors; its list did not match what A said in its own filings. I then asked for data on the four publicly traded peers A listed in its filings.

The results: Gemini was correct each time on shares outstanding; close to right each time on price; right 4 times out of 5 on book value (and way off on one); close to right 3 times out of 5 on trailing revenue; and fairly poor on profitability metrics and net debt numbers. In other words, you have to accept a large degree of error to find that useful.

There are valuable uses to Gen AI: summarization, transcribing, translation. I’m not sure, though, that summarization is that useful in the research process. If a company is easily summarized, the value added from Gen AI is low; if a company is hard to summarize, it deserves the closer reading to grasp details. One doesn’t need to get investment ideas from arcane corners of company filings to benefit from a broader and deeper understanding of a given company.

Successful investing, the way we attempt it, is about identifying opportunities other people are not seeing properly. That is on the one hand hard to do with a tool that aggregates and approximates text. On the other hand, if that tool does a good job, it saves time for the decision making that is the core to successful investing.

I’ve just started experimenting, but haven’t unlocked anything yet. Suggestions are welcome.

AI as an investment theme

How are we investing in artificial intelligence? Cautiously.

I’ve said before that I both don’t believe in and am naturally predisposed to not chase trends. At the same time, artificial intelligence is making some sort of impact on companies across the market. We like boring companies, but also will take opportunities where we find them.

Everus Construction Group (ECG) is a good example; I invested because it was a spin-off that got oversold after its own and a peer’s Q4 earnings report. It also, as a construction company that also builds out power lines, benefits from the data center boom fueled by AI. The company’s performance has been very strong so far this year, its guidance for the second half of the year seems improbably low. At the same time, the follow through on this demand is yet to be proven, and this may not be a forever winner.

Big tech companies play in and are obviously affected by AI. We own Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), and a small position of Microsoft (MSFT); of those, Microsoft has been seen as a winner due to its association with OpenAI. But really, the story seems to be that Microsoft’s Azure is gaining ground on Amazon Web services. AI is the source of demand driving the continued growth.

We invested in Alphabet (GOOG) earlier this year when the market viewed it as an AI loser, which seemed unlikely to me. Gemini has only grown in prominence since then. The other part of this thesis was that a government break up of Alphabet would be good for shareholders. The market disagrees; but after the recent anti-trust ruling did not force Google to sell its Chrome unit, the stock has gone fine. Waymo, Alphabet’s autonomous driving unit, is also an AI-powered company that is deploying fleets in more and more cities. Not GenAI, exactly, but in the ballpark.

Which gets to some of our ‘that’s also sort of AI’ investments. Trip Advisor (TRIP) has a motherlode of user-generated content in its travel reviews. It could productize that better with summarization tools, or license the text to an LLM developer. Lyft (LYFT) recently announced a partnership with Waymo. Whether that is a good thing or a threat is to be determined.

Semiconductor companies are the ‘picks and shovels’ of AI. Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) is the bedrock manufacturer of semiconductors, while Broadcom (AVGO) is increasingly viewed as the non-Nvidia winner in chip designers. Broadcom trades at roughly 85x trailing earnings, 65x trailing free cash flow, and 36x next year’s earnings. I have never owned such a bubbly large cap stock. We trim the position regularly, leery of booking too large a tax hit, and also aware that Broadcom is a great company, both profitable and fast-growing. It is also tremendously valued.

AI and Society

I had wanted to talk about AI’s broader societal impact, but I’m not sure that my views are all that relevant in a professional capacity. I have concerns: the rash way the corporate class wants to implement AI and cut jobs; the environmental cost; the effect on education and on children. These will seep into our investing thinking and how we run our business, but they’re background.

I lived through the internet bubble as a high schooler. I had no investing interest at the time, but could see the adoption of the internet picking up. AOL instant messenger, eBay auctions, ESPN.go.com, and Napster were gradual steps towards the always plugged in world. It was a novelty, but it was clear it could become more.

AI has been different. The potential became apparent as soon as Chat GPT was launched in 2022. It has real value to some people: many of my non-native English-speaking acquaintances love it as a way to write in English. Likewise, it’s valuable for us to summarize and synthesize text we’re working on in Spanish. Stepping out of generative AI, I can point to a friend who works on movies and raves about AI in sound editing. Peter Jackson used AI to edit Get Back, the Beatles documentary, for example, to isolate sound much better.

All of which is to say, if we call AI the cutting edge of modern software and computing technology, it’s no surprise that some of what it does will make things easier to do, and some of it is hype. Many key participants have admitted AI is in a bubble phase. The speed with which we’ve tried to use AI could make it harder to profit from. Whether there is just a slowdown or a more acute bursting of the bubble, it seems likely some sort of reckoning will come.

Part of investing is knowing thyself. I start from skepticism and value investing principles. I work to stay open minded, and will with AI, but almost three years on from the major Chat GPT model launch, the final real impact seems an open question to me. We will continue to balance caution with looking for ideas, and prepare our portfolio for both good and bad market environments. A natural inclination.
